votes.reason: The Python code correctly checks if there are two distinct elements in the list that sum to zero, which exactly matches the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly checks if there are two distinct elements in the list that sum to zero, matching the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly checks if there are two distinct elements in the list that sum to zero, which exactly matches the Coq specification.

The Coq spec says res=true iff there exist distinct indices i,j with l[i]+l[j]=0. The Python code checks all pairs (i,j) with i<j for l[i]+l[j]==0, which covers all distinct pairs since if i!=j and l[i]+l[j]=0, then either i<j or j<i, and in either case the pair sums to 0. The Python returns whether res matches the existence of such a pair, which is equivalent to the Coq biconditional on concrete inputs.

Both characterize that res is true exactly when there exist two distinct indices i != j with l[i] + l[j] = 0. The Python checks all i<j pairs (sufficient for existence with i≠j) and returns res == has_pair, matching the Coq iff.

Both compute whether there exists a pair of distinct indices with elements summing to zero and check res matches that. The Python searches pairs with j>i, which is equivalent to i≠j for existence, so results coincide for all inputs.
Python checks for existence of two distinct indices i<j with l[i]+l[j]==0 and returns whether res matches this, which is equivalent to the Coq spec res=true <-> exists i≠j with vi+vj=0.
